hero.summary: Acquisition of Turkish Citizenship After 5 Years of Continuous Residence in Turkey

intro.title: Application for Turkish Citizenship by General Route
paragraphs: One of the ways to subsequently acquire Turkish citizenship is for foreigners who reside continuously in Turkey for a certain period to apply under general provisions. Unlike citizenship applications through investment, the general route is not based on a specific investment amount but on the applicant having a legal, continuous residence history showing intent to settle in Turkey.

Under Article 11 of the Turkish Citizenship Law No. 5901, a foreigner wishing to acquire Turkish citizenship through the general route must have resided continuously in Turkey for five years backward from the application date. However, completing the five-year residence period alone does not grant citizenship. The applicant must also be of age and have capacity, show intent to settle in Turkey through behavior, not have a disease posing a danger to public health, be of good moral character, speak adequate Turkish, have an income or profession to support themselves and dependents, and not have any condition posing an obstacle to national security and public order.

This application route is particularly important for foreigners working with a work permit, living with a family residence permit, holding a long-term residence permit, owning real estate or carrying out commercial activities, having established a family order, or showing a permanent settlement intent in Turkey.

This guide is prepared to explain the residence period, eligible permit types, day counting, application documents, who the application covers, and key aspects to consider in the citizenship process for foreigners wishing to apply for Turkish citizenship by general route after a five-year residence permit.
This guide is for informational purposes only. Please consult a qualified attorney for legal advice.

process.title: Steps to Apply for Turkish Citizenship by General Route
process.description: All steps of the general route citizenship application consisting of 15 sections:

items.title: SECTION 1 - LEGAL BASIS OF THE CITIZENSHIP APPLICATION BY GENERAL ROUTE
items.desc:
The application for Turkish citizenship by general route is regulated under Article 11 of the Turkish Citizenship Law No. 5901 and the Regulation on the Implementation of the Turkish Citizenship Law. In this type of application, citizenship is not acquired directly or automatically. The applicant must document that they meet the conditions, and the application must be deemed appropriate as a result of the review by administrative authorities.

The basic requirement is to have resided continuously in Turkey for five years backward from the application date. However, it is not enough for this period to expire chronologically. The residence must be legal, demonstrate intent to settle, be based on accepted residence types, and not be interrupted by periods spent abroad.

Therefore, before applying, the applicant's last five years of residence history, permit types, entry-exit records, work or residence permit periods, address registration, and family status must be evaluated together.

items.title: SECTION 2 - REQUIREMENTS FOR TURKISH CITIZENSHIP BY GENERAL ROUTE
items.desc:
The basic requirements for Turkish citizenship by general route are as follows:
• Being of age and having capacity under their national law, or Turkish Civil Code if stateless,
• Having resided continuously in Turkey for five years backward from the application date,
• Confirming by behavior the decision to settle in Turkey,
• Not having a disease posing a danger to public health,
• Being of good moral character,
• Speaking Turkish at a level to adapt to social life,
• Having an income or profession to support themselves and dependents,
• Having no condition posing an obstacle to national security and public order.

All these conditions are evaluated together. The application of every foreigner who completes the five-year residence period is not automatically accepted. The administration examines the applicant's physical links with Turkey, social integration, income status, criminal record, public order status, and permanent settlement intent.

items.title: SECTION 3 - FIVE-YEAR RESIDENCE REQUIREMENT
items.desc:
To apply for Turkish citizenship by general route, the applicant must have resided continuously in Turkey for five years backward from the application date. This period is calculated backward from the application date, not forward.

For example, if the application date is July 1, 2026, the applicant is expected to have a continuous and legal residence history in Turkey since July 1, 2021. During this period, there must be no long gaps between residence permits, the applicant must not fall into visa violation or unauthorized stay, and periods spent abroad must not reach a level that interrupts the residence.

In calculating the five-year period, not only the days physically spent in Turkey are important, but also the legal status on which this stay is based. Periods spent in Turkey without a legal residence permit are not accepted as valid residence for the application.

items.title: SECTION 4 - DAY COUNT AND PERIODS SPENT ABROAD
items.desc:
One of the most important issues in general route applications is whether the periods spent by the applicant outside Turkey interrupt the residence requirement. The applicant's entry and exit records to Turkey within the five-year period backward from the application date are examined.

When performing the day count in practice, special attention should be paid to:
• Total period spent outside Turkey in the last five years,
• Total period spent outside Turkey in the last year,
• Whether stays abroad are based on justified reasons such as mandatory public service, education, or health,
• Whether the foreigner holds a valid residence or work permit in Turkey,
• Gaps between residence permits,
• Whether the foreigner's physical links showing intent to settle continue.

In terms of general residence law, stays outside Turkey exceeding six months in a year or exceeding one year in total in the last five years (excluding mandatory service, education, and health) can be considered as an interruption of residence. Therefore, applicants should avoid staying abroad for more than one year in the last five years and six months in the last year.

However, administrative practice in citizenship applications can be more conservative. In the Civil Registration and Citizenship practice, if the total period spent abroad within the required residence period exceeds six months, it may be evaluated that the residence is interrupted. Hence, entry-exit records must be analyzed in detail before the application.

items.title: SECTION 5 - WHICH RESIDENCE PERMITS ARE DEEMED ELIGIBLE?
items.desc:
In applications by general route, not every residence permit type is automatically accepted. A legal residence permit is required, and it must show an intent to settle in Turkey.

Generally, permit types that show intent to settle are:
• Work permit,
• Family residence permit,
• Long-term residence permit,
• Short-term residence permit based on owning real estate,
• Short-term residence permits showing business connections, starting a business, investment, or similar settlement intent,
• Residence permits showing a family setup in Turkey,
• Other legal residence permits establishing a basis for long-term and continuous living.

Since a work permit also replaces a residence permit by law, the periods spent with a work permit are critical. However, physical presence, stays abroad, and gaps between permits must be examined.

Conversely, some residence types that do not show intent to settle may not be accepted. In particular, tourist residence permits, student residence permits, permits for accompanying a student child, treatment permits, stays based on asylum applications, and diplomatic IDs may not be counted.

Therefore, it is not enough for the applicant to just be in Turkey for five years. The type of permit and whether it will count must be evaluated.

items.title: SECTION 6 - TOURIST AND STUDENT RESIDENCE PERMITS
items.desc:
A tourist residence permit is one of the most common issues in general route applications because it is generally not evaluated as showing intent to settle. Therefore, staying in Turkey for five years solely with a tourist residence permit is not accepted. Furthermore, having a tourist permit even for a short period after holding accepted permits (family, property, etc.) can reset the previous periods, acting as if the applicant has no prior eligible stays.

Similar care is required for student residence permits. Stays with a student permit may not be accepted as showing direct intent to settle. However, if the applicant completes their education in Turkey and transitions to an accepted status like a work permit, family residence, property, or long-term residence, it can be evaluated based on the case. Student permit periods are calculated towards the 5-year requirement, but holding an active student permit at the time of application is an obstacle; the applicant must have transitioned to an eligible permit or work permit.

Therefore, the residence history of those who stayed with tourist or student permits must be examined in detail before applying.

items.title: SECTION 7 - REQUIREMENT OF VALID PERMIT AT THE TIME OF APPLICATION
items.desc:
When applying for citizenship by general route, the applicant must hold a valid residence or work permit. Even if the five-year residence requirement is met, the absence of a valid permit at the moment of application prevents the file from being processed.

An ikamet card with sufficient duration to conclude citizenship transactions is sought. If the permit is close to expiry, renewal should be planned before the citizenship application.

For foreigners with a work permit, a separate residence permit is not required. However, the work permit must be valid, active at the time of application, and have no gaps in previous periods.

If the legal status has expired, no renewal has been made, or there is a violation, it creates a severe risk. Applications with less than 1 month remaining are generally not accepted by the Civil Registration and Citizenship offices.

At this stage, the most critical requirement is holding one of the accepted permit types (property (b), family (a), long-term, or work permit) during the day count and application process. Otherwise, it is not possible to request a day count or apply.

items.title: SECTION 8 - DEMONSTRATING INTENT TO SETTLE IN TURKEY
items.desc:
It is not enough to just reside for five years; the applicant must show intent to settle through behavior.

Situations demonstrating this intent include:
• Acquiring real estate in Turkey,
• Starting a business in Turkey,
• Making an investment,
• Transferring commercial activity to Turkey,
• Working with a work permit,
• Residing with family in Turkey,
• Being married to a Turkish citizen,
• Having parents, siblings, or children who already acquired citizenship,
• Completing education in Turkey,
• Living based on a long-term residence permit,
• Establishing social, economic, and family ties in Turkey.

Not all of these need to be present simultaneously, but the applicant must be able to show they are in Turkey to settle permanently, not for temporary or tourist purposes.

items.title: SECTION 9 - INCOME, PROFESSION, AND LIVELIHOOD
items.desc:
The applicant must have an income or profession to support themselves and dependents in Turkey.

This requirement ensures the applicant can live without social assistance. The following documents can prove livelihood:
• Work permit,
• Salary slip,
• Employment contract,
• Tax plate,
• Company partnership documents,
• Bank account statements,
• Property rental income,
• Pension income,
• Other documents showing regular income.

The income must be regular, sustainable, and aligned with the applicant's family obligations. Unexplained or temporary money transfers may not be deemed sufficient.

items.title: SECTION 10 - TURKISH LANGUAGE PROFICIENCY AND INTERVIEW
items.desc:
The applicant must speak Turkish at a level to adapt to social life. This does not require academic Turkish, but the applicant is expected to express themselves, answer simple questions, and communicate basic needs.

During the process, the applicant is interviewed by the citizenship evaluation commission. The interview evaluates language skills, life in Turkey, family, profession, income, settlement intent, and social circle.

It is crucial that answers align with the documents. Applicants who do not physically live in Turkey, have discrepancies in address registration, or fail to show sufficient settlement intent face risk.

items.title: SECTION 11 - WHO THE APPLICATION COVERS
items.desc:
An application by general route primarily yields results for the applicant. Acquisition of citizenship does not automatically affect the spouse's citizenship. Therefore, the spouse does not automatically become a citizen.

Minor children are evaluated separately. If one parent acquires citizenship, minor children under their custody can acquire citizenship with the other parent's consent. If both parents acquire citizenship, minor children can also acquire it under certain conditions.

Adult children do not acquire citizenship automatically based on their parents. Since their status changes if they become of age during the process, they may need to file a separate application.

Therefore, family members, children's ages, custody, consent of the other parent, and family documents must be evaluated before applying.

items.title: SECTION 12 - REQUIRED DOCUMENTS FOR APPLICATION
items.desc:
Generally, the following documents are prepared for the application:
• Application form,
• Biometric photograph,
• Passport or equivalent document,
• Document showing statelessness (if applicable),
• Birth certificate or official identity document,
• Marital status document,
• Marriage certificate (if married),
• Divorce decree (if divorced),
• Death certificate of spouse (if widowed),
• Population register extract showing family ties,
• Health report showing no disease posing a public health danger,
• Documents showing income or profession (work permit, bank records),
• Entry-exit records showing 5 years of continuous residence,
• Valid residence or work permit at the time of application,
• Certified copy of court decisions (if any),
• Service fee receipt,
• Additional documents if requested.

Documents from foreign countries must have an apostille or consular approval, and certified Turkish translations must be prepared. Names, birth details, and family links must be consistent.

items.title: SECTION 13 - APPLICATION AUTHORITY AND WORKFLOW
items.desc:
Applications are filed with the Provincial Directorate of Civil Registration and Citizenship of the place of residence in Turkey. The application must be made in person or via a power of attorney containing special authority. Postal applications are not accepted.

The workflow generally consists of:
1. Evaluating residence history and eligibility,
2. Examining entry-exit records and permits,
3. Checking valid permit status at the time of application,
4. Preparing documents,
5. Filing the application,
6. Giving fingerprints at the police,
7. Preliminary review of the file,
8. Completing missing documents,
9. Interview by the commission,
10. Security and background checks,
11. Sending the file to the Ministry of Interior,
12. Evaluation by administrative authorities,
13. Acquisition of citizenship if approved.

Processing the file does not guarantee citizenship. Authorities evaluate if all conditions are fully met.

items.title: SECTION 14 - APPLICATION RISKS AND REASONS FOR REJECTION
items.desc:
The most common risks are:
• The 5-year residence not being continuous,
• Long stays abroad in the last five years,
• Staying abroad for more than six months in the last year,
• Gaps between residence permits,
• Mistakenly assuming tourist permits count towards the requirement,
• Lacking a valid permit at the moment of application,
• Insufficient settlement intent,
• Inability to prove livelihood/income,
• Insufficient Turkish in the interview,
• Having a criminal record or ongoing trial,
• Negative security assessment,
• Inconsistent identity or marital details,
• Discretionary power of the administration.

Due to these risks, entry-exit records must be calculated day-by-day and timed correctly before applying.

items.title: SECTION 15 - IMPORTANCE OF LEGAL ASSISTANCE
items.desc:
Applying by general route is not just about filling forms and submitting papers. Residence periods, entry-exit records, permit types, work permits, income, and public order risks must be evaluated together.

Attorney support is crucial to:
• Calculating the 5-year residence,
• Evaluating stays abroad,
• Separating eligible and ineligible permit types,
• Verifying valid permits during day counting and application,
• Assessing family coverage,
• Preparing documents,
• Coordinating foreign document apostilles, translations, and notary procedures,
• Ensuring file consistency before the interview,
• Handling requests for additional documents,
• Evaluating administrative appeals or lawsuits if rejected.

Incomplete or incorrect applications lead to rejection. Professional legal assistance is highly recommended from the start.

processStages.title: General Stages of the Citizenship Application by General Route
items: 1. Review of the applicant's residence history
2. Evaluation of entry-exit records of the last five years
4. Verification of permit types for citizenship eligibility
5. Checking valid permit status at the moment of application
6. Preparing documents demonstrating intent to settle
7. Completing income, profession, health, and family documents
8. Submitting the application to the Civil Registration and Citizenship Directorate
10. Preliminary review, interview, and commission procedures
12. Evaluation of the file by authorized authorities
13. Acquisition of Turkish citizenship if approved

items.title: Turkish Citizenship Law No. 5901
items.text: Regulates the acquisition, loss, and general procedures of Turkish citizenship.

items.title: Article 11 of the Turkish Citizenship Law No. 5901
items.text: Regulates the requirements for acquiring Turkish citizenship by general route.

items.title: Regulation on the Implementation of the Turkish Citizenship Law
items.text: Regulates the procedures and principles of the application by general route.

items.title: Law No. 6458 on Foreigners and International Protection
items.text: Important in terms of residence permits, work permits replacing residence, and residence interruption.
